Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to set aside time in the evenings after my part-time job, but it rarely worked out as planned. I'm a visual learner, so I'd often spend what little time I had creating diagrams and color-coded notes rather than just reading through materials. With only about 14 hours a week to study and everything else going on, I had to be strategic, but I'll admit I wasn't always successful at sticking to any real schedule.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what shifts I had at work. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything academic, then I'd have a burst of activity when deadlines were approaching. I wanted to be more consistent, but when you're dealing with limited resources and trying to help out at home financially, regular study times become a luxury. The anxiety of falling behind would build up, and then I'd try to cram everything in at once.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or seeing a notification on my phone that made me log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was something urgent I was missing because the anxiety of not knowing was worse than actually seeing bad news. I also logged in when I had a few minutes between other responsibilities, just to feel like I was staying connected to the course somehow.
Q4: For a few weeks in the middle of the semester, I did get into a rhythm of checking the LMS every morning before my day got too busy. The gamified task system actually helped with that because it gave me small goals to hit, which felt manageable. But then things got heavier with work and family stuff, and that routine fell apart. I kept trying to rebuild it, but it never stuck the same way again.
Q5: The video lectures were honestly the most useful for me because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the visual content at my own pace. I watched over four hours of video content because that format just clicks with my brain better than reading dense text. The gamified tasks also helped because they broke things down into smaller pieces that didn't feel as overwhelming when I was already stressed.
Q6: I barely touched the discussion forums, only posting once the whole semester. It felt awkward putting my thoughts out there when I wasn't confident I understood the material well enough. The lengthy reading materials I often skipped or skimmed because I didn't have the time or mental energy to process them properly. I knew I was missing out, but something had to give.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate progress or when it directly connected to something I'd be tested on. The gamified elements helped because earning points or completing a challenge gave me a small sense of accomplishment even on bad days. If something felt too abstract or disconnected from the exam, I'd deprioritize it even if it might have been valuable in the long run.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a trap to me, honestly. Part of me wanted to do them because I knew they'd help, but when you're already drowning in required work and life responsibilities, optional stuff becomes the first thing you cut. Required activities carried the weight of consequences, so they got my attention even when I was exhausted. Optional ones just made me feel guilty for not doing them.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and videos when I had good internet access, then study them later at home or during breaks at work. I also used YouTube videos and free resources I found online to supplement what I didn't understand from class. My notes from those sessions were extensive, but none of that showed up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were many times I'd be working through problems on paper or reviewing my handwritten notes for hours without logging into anything. I'd also study with a friend from another section who explained concepts to me in ways that clicked better. All that effort was real learning, but if you looked at my LMS activity for those days, it would show nothing at all.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access a video or document once, download it or screenshot the important parts, and then work with those materials offline for days. Internet access at home isn't always reliable, so I learned to grab what I needed when I could and then study independently. The LMS would only show that initial access, not the hours I spent with the material afterward.
Q12: Around midterms, everything crashed down on me at once. My anxiety was through the roof, I was picking up extra shifts because we needed the money, and I just couldn't keep up with the pace of new material. I'd stare at the screen sometimes and nothing would register. The resilience I usually pride myself on felt stretched to its breaking point during those weeks.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the LMS became something I'd dread opening because it just reminded me of everything I was behind on. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely sometimes, which only made things worse. Competing responsibilities meant I was constantly triaging what absolutely had to get done versus what I could let slide.
Q14: There were plenty of times I'd have the LMS open but I was just going through the motions. I'd scroll through content without really processing it because my mind was elsewhere, worrying about other things. Sometimes I'd watch videos at 2x speed just to technically complete them, but I retained almost nothing. The activity was there, but the learning wasn't.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety would build up around them, making them feel bigger and scarier than they probably were. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I was less tired or less stressed, but tomorrow always brought its own problems. By the time I finally attempted things, it was often too late to do them well.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit anything. I'd have bursts of frantic activity right before something was due, trying to pull together whatever I could. Without that external pressure, I'd keep pushing things off indefinitely. The problem was that last-minute work was never my best work, and my assignment completion rate shows that clearly.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it did motivate me to try harder on the next thing. But feedback often came too late for me to really use it, or I was already so far behind that it felt like criticism of past failures rather than guidance for improvement. Positive feedback on my assignment scores when I did submit gave me hope that I could turn things around, even though I ultimately couldn't.
Q18: The course structure felt like it assumed everyone had the same amount of time and resources to dedicate to it. The pacing was relentless, and if you fell behind, catching up felt nearly impossible. I appreciated that there were gamified elements that tried to make things more engaging, but the overall structure didn't leave much room for someone in my situation to recover from setbacks.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier because they gave me achievable micro-goals when everything else felt overwhelming. But the sheer volume of content and the attendance requirements made things harder. Missing classes because of work meant I was always playing catch-up, and the system didn't seem designed for students who couldn't be there every time.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well, actually. High activity might mean someone is trying really hard, logging in constantly, watching videos, checking things, but still struggling to actually complete and submit work. The gap between engaging with content and producing finished assignments is huge when you're dealing with anxiety, time constraints, and confusion about the material.
Q21: I can see how that happens too. Some students might be efficient learners who don't need to spend hours in the LMS to understand things. They might use textbooks, study groups, or just be naturally quick at grasping concepts. For me, I tried to be efficient out of necessity, but my exam scores show that approach didn't work as well as I needed it to.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active, how often you log in, what you click on. But they miss everything that happens in your head and outside the system. They can't measure understanding, they can't see the offline work, and they definitely can't capture the anxiety that makes you stare at a page for an hour without absorbing anything. Numbers tell a partial story at best.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material and putting in genuine effort to understand it, not just going through the motions. For me, it means those moments when concepts click and I feel like I'm growing intellectually, even if briefly. It's not about how many times I logged in but whether my mind was truly present and working when I was studying.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was fighting to stay in this course despite everything working against me. It wouldn't show the late nights studying from downloaded materials, the anxiety attacks before logging in, or the determination to keep trying even after failing. It sees my low completion rates and attendance, but it doesn't see a student who refused to give up even when giving up would have been easier.
